Home.seoTitle: Decmatrix | Interactive Math Calculators and Algorithm Simulators
Home.seoDescription: Explore interactive tools for data structures, linear algebra, precalculus, and financial math. Visualize algorithms and solve calculations step by step.

title.mainTitle: <retroText>Interactive Tools</retroText> for Algorithms and Computational Math.
title.subTitle01: Solve mathematical problems and visualize algorithms in real time.

introduction.intro: Decmatrix is a platform of mathematical and computational tools designed to simplify complex calculations and assist in data-driven decision making in a clear, practical, and accessible way.
introduction.idea: From financial planning to binary tree simulators, through equations, functions, and fundamental data structure concepts, Decmatrix aims to bring together intuitive explanations and interactive calculators that help transform theory into real-world application. As time goes on, more tools are added to Decmatrix, always focusing on usability and clarity.

clarityText.title: Built for clarity
clarityText.description: Our tools are designed to provide clear and concise results, making it easy for users to understand complex mathematical concepts and computations.

previewBinaryTree.title: Preview of our Binary Search Tree Simulator
previewBinaryTree.description: Interact with the preview of our binary search tree simulator. Click 'Remove value' to follow step-by-step how the tree restructures itself.

dataStructuresSection.title: Data Structures
dataStructuresSection.description: Interactive BST and AVL simulators to visualize how data structures work in practice.

precalculusSection.title: Precalculus
precalculusSection.description: Solve equations and explore algebraic concepts with step-by-step interactive tools.

financialCalculatorsSection.title: Financial Calculators
financialCalculatorsSection.description: Plan and simulate financial scenarios with intuitive interest and investment tools.

linearAlgebraSection.title: Linear Algebra
linearAlgebraSection.description: Perform matrix operations instantly and understand linear algebra visually.

avaliableTools.title: Available Tools
avaliableTools.subtitle: Choose a tool and learn by doing

secondGradeEquationCard.title: Quadratic Equation Calculator
secondGradeEquationCard.description: Solve quadratic equations quickly and easily, with step-by-step explanations.

compoundInterestCalculatorCard.title: Compound Interest Calculator
compoundInterestCalculatorCard.description: Calculate the growth of your investment over time with compound interest, considering periodic contributions.

simpleInterestCalculatorCard.title: Simple Interest Calculator
simpleInterestCalculatorCard.description: Calculate simple interest quickly and easily, ideal for basic financial planning.

binarySearchTreeSimulatorCard.title: Binary Search Tree Simulator
binarySearchTreeSimulatorCard.description: Binary search tree (BST) simulator that allows you to insert, remove, and visualize the tree structure in real-time, facilitating the understanding of its operation and properties.

avlTreeSimulatorCard.title: AVL Tree Simulator
avlTreeSimulatorCard.description: AVL tree simulator that allows you to insert, remove, and visualize the balanced tree structure in real-time, helping to understand how AVL trees maintain balance during insertion and removal operations.

matrixMultiplication.title: Matrix Multiplier
matrixMultiplication.description: Matrix multiplier that allows you to perform multiplication operations between matrices, with support for matrices of different dimensions.

matrixBasicOperations.title: Basic Matrix Operations
matrixBasicOperations.description: Calculator for basic matrix operations, addition and subtraction. Additionally, it allows transposing and inverting matrices, with support for matrices of different dimensions.

bubbleSortSimulatorCard.title: Bubble Sort Simulator
bubbleSortSimulatorCard.description: Step-by-step Bubble Sort visualizer with animated bars.

insertionSortSimulatorCard.title: Insertion Sort Simulator
insertionSortSimulatorCard.description: See how Insertion Sort places each element in the correct position.

selectionSortSimulatorCard.title: Selection Sort Simulator
selectionSortSimulatorCard.description: Follow Selection Sort picking the smallest value each round.

objectivesSection.title: Our Objectives
objectivesSection.description: The objective of Decmatrix is to democratize access to mathematical and computational tools, making complex concepts more accessible and understandable for everyone. We value clarity, practicality, and applicability, always seeking to offer solutions that truly make a difference for our users.

valuesSection.title: Our Values
values: Clarity: Present information in a simple and straightforward manner, facilitating understanding.
Practicality: Develop useful tools that can be applied in users' daily lives.
Accessibility: Ensure that our tools are accessible to everyone, regardless of prior knowledge level.
Innovation: Constantly seek new ways to improve and expand our offerings, incorporating user feedback and technological advances.

faqSection.question: 01. What mathematical tools and simulators does Decmatrix offer?
faqSection.answer: Decmatrix is a free platform focused on students of exact sciences, engineering, and computer science. We offer interactive simulators for data structures (such as Binary Search Tree - BST and self-balancing AVL Tree), financial calculators for simple and compound interest, step-by-step solutions for second-degree equations (pre-calculus), linear algebra tools for matrix multiplication and basic operations, and more.

faqSection.question: 02. How do algorithm simulators and calculators help in studies?
faqSection.answer: Our focus is real-time visualization. Instead of just delivering the final result, Decmatrix details the calculation step by step. You can visualize AVL trees, understand the mathematical logic behind matrix addition and inversion, and analyze detailed graphs of financial investment growth.

faqSection.question: 03. Didn't find the calculator or structural algorithm you needed?
faqSection.answer: Decmatrix is a constantly evolving project. If you have ideas for new tools, found an error on any page, or want to suggest improvements to the website, send your feedback via email decimatrix25@gmail.com. Your suggestion is always welcome!

AVLTreeBuilder.seoDescription: Learn data structures in practice with our Interactive AVL Tree Simulator. Insert values, visualize balancing, and understand rotations step by step.

definition.title: AVL Tree - Definition
definition.intro: An AVL tree (Adelson-Velsky and Landis) is a self-balancing binary search tree where the height difference between the left and right subtrees of any node is at most 1. This ensures that the tree remains approximately balanced, providing efficient insertion, removal, and search operations.
definition.curiosity: AVL trees were the first self-balancing binary search trees invented, in 1962, by Georgy Adelson-Velsky and Evgenii Landis. They are widely used in applications requiring fast search and update operations, such as databases and file systems.
definition.rememberHighlight: Remember: A binary tree is, by definition, a set of nodes that is either empty or consists of a root and two disjoint binary subtrees, called the left and right subtrees of the root.

proprieties.title: AVL Tree - Properties
proprieties.intro: The main properties of an AVL tree include: automatic balancing, which keeps the tree height at O(log n), ensuring efficient operations; the ordering of elements, facilitating quick searches; and the hierarchical structure that organizes data efficiently. Rotations (single and double) are used to restore balance after insertions and removals.

operations.title: AVL Tree - Search, Insertion and Removal
operations.searchText: Search in an AVL tree is similar to search in a standard binary search tree, leveraging the ordering of elements to quickly locate the desired value.
operations.insertionText: Insertion in an AVL tree starts like in a binary search tree, locating the correct position for the new node. After insertion, it is necessary to check the balance of the tree and, if needed, perform rotations to restore balance. To understand insertion operations in binary search trees, you can visit our <linkBTS>binary search tree simulator</linkBTS>.
operations.removalText: Removal in an AVL tree also follows the process of a binary search tree, but after removal, it is necessary to check and restore the balance of the tree, if needed, using appropriate rotations.

balanceAndRotations.title: AVL Tree - Balance and Rotations
balanceAndRotations.intro: Balance in an AVL tree is maintained by calculating the balance factor for each node, which is the difference between the heights of the left and right subtrees. If the balance factor of a node becomes greater than 1 or less than -1 after an insertion or removal, the tree is unbalanced and needs to be corrected.

cases.intro: There are four main cases of imbalance that can occur in an AVL tree, each requiring a specific rotation to restore balance:
cases.case01: Right single rotation: Occurs when a node has a balance factor greater than 1 and its left child has a balance factor of 1 or 0.
cases.case02: Left single rotation: Occurs when a node has a balance factor less than -1 and its right child has a balance factor of -1 or 0.
cases.case03: Left double rotation: Occurs when a node has a balance factor greater than 1 and its left child has a balance factor less than 0.
cases.case04: Right double rotation: Occurs when a node has a balance factor less than -1 and its right child has a balance factor greater than 0.

faqSection.question: 01. What is an AVL tree and what is it used for?
faqSection.answer: An AVL tree is a self-balancing binary search tree. Its main rule is that the height difference between the left and right subtrees of any node (the balance factor) can never be greater than 1. This guarantees that searches, insertions, and removals remain efficient.

faqSection.question: 02. How does an AVL tree perform its automatic balancing?
faqSection.answer: The system calculates the balance factor of each node. If, after you insert or remove a value, this factor goes beyond the allowed range (becoming greater than 1 or less than -1), the tree automatically performs structural rotations to restore its balance.

faqSection.question: 03. What are rotations in an AVL tree and how do they work?
faqSection.answer: They are the operations that correct imbalances. There are four main types of rotation: single right, single left, double left, and double right. The algorithm chooses which one to use depending exactly on where the imbalance occurred in the structure.

BSTTreeBuilder.seoDescription: Build and visualize Binary Search Trees in real time. Insert and remove values, follow each step, and understand BST properties and operations.
BSTTreeBuilder.bstDefinitionText01: A BST (Binary Search Tree) is a binary search tree where each node has at most two children, and for each node, all values in the left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in the right subtree are greater. This allows efficient search, insertion, and removal operations.
BSTTreeBuilder.bstDefinitionText02: Remember: A binary tree is, by definition, a set of nodes that is either empty or consists of a root and two disjoint binary subtrees, called the left and right subtrees of the root.
BSTTreeBuilder.bstProprietiesText01: The main properties of a BST tree include: the ordering of elements, efficiency in search, insertion, and removal operations (on average O(log n)), and the hierarchical structure that facilitates data organization. However, performance can degrade to O(n) in cases of degenerate trees, trees where nodes all fall to one side.
BSTTreeBuilder.bstSearchInsertionAndRemovalTitle: BST Tree - Search, Insertion, and Removal
BSTTreeBuilder.bstSearchText: Searching in a BST tree starts at the root and traverses the tree by comparing the searched value with the current node's value, moving left or right as needed. The algorithm is very simple and efficient, being O(log n) on average.
BSTTreeBuilder.bstInsertionText: Inserting a new value in the BST tree follows a process similar to searching, locating the correct position for the new node and always inserting it as a leaf.
BSTTreeBuilder.bstRemovalText: Removing a node in a BST tree can be more complex, depending on the number of children the node to be removed has. Nodes without children can be removed directly, nodes with one child can be replaced by their child, and nodes with two children require replacement by the in-order successor or predecessor.

faqSection.question: 01. What is the main rule of a Binary Search Tree (BST)?
faqSection.answer: In a BST, each node can have at most two children. The rule is: all values in the left subtree are smaller than the parent node's value, and all values in the right subtree are greater.

faqSection.question: 02. What is the disadvantage or worst-case scenario of a BST?
faqSection.answer: Usually it is very efficient (taking logarithmic time on average). However, if you insert already sorted data, all nodes can fall to one side. This creates a 'degenerate tree', destroying efficiency and making search take as long as in a simple list.

faqSection.question: 03. How does the removal of a node work in a BST?
faqSection.answer: It depends on the position of the node. If it is a 'leaf' (without children), it can be simply deleted. If it has one child, the child replaces it. If the node has two children, the logic is more complex: it must be replaced by its successor or predecessor in tree order.

CompoundInterestCalculator.seoDescription: Simulate compound interest with initial capital and monthly contributions. See detailed summaries and charts of your investment growth over time.
CompoundInterestCalculator.alertWrongValues: Please enter valid values for all fields.

inputCapital.label: Initial Capital ($)
inputCapital.info: The initial capital is the amount you will invest or lend at the beginning of the period.

inputInterest.label: Interest Rate (%)
inputInterest.info: The interest rate is the percentage that will be applied to the initial capital during the period. It can be monthly or yearly.

inputPeriod.label: Period (t)
inputPeriod.info: The period is the duration of the investment or loan, in months or years, depending on the selected interest rate.

inputContribution.label: Monthly Contribution
inputContribution.info: The monthly contribution is the amount you add to the initial capital each month during the investment or loan period.

CompoundInterestCalculator.alertValueTooHigh: The final amount of the investment is too high. Please enter smaller values.
CompoundInterestCalculator.alertPeriodTooLong: The maximum allowed period is 50 years (600 months).

usageTutorial.title: How to Use the Compound Interest Calculator
usageTutorial.intro: To use the Compound Interest Calculator, follow the steps below:
steps: Enter the initial capital you want to invest or lend in the Initial Capital ($) field.
Enter the interest rate in the Interest Rate (%) field and select whether it is monthly or yearly.
Set the investment or loan period in the Period (t) field and choose whether it is in months or years.
If desired, enter a monthly contribution in the Monthly Contribution ($) field.
Click the Calculate Compound Interest button to see the detailed results of your investment.
usageTutorial.step01: Enter the initial capital you want to invest or lend in the Initial Capital ($) field.
usageTutorial.step02: Enter the interest rate in the Interest Rate (%) field and select whether it is monthly or yearly.
usageTutorial.step03: Set the investment or loan period in the Period (t) field and choose whether it is in months or years.
usageTutorial.step04: If desired, enter a monthly contribution in the Monthly Contribution ($) field.
usageTutorial.step05: Click the Calculate Compound Interest button to see the detailed results of your investment.
usageTutorial.conclusion: The calculator will provide an investment summary, including the total invested, total interest earned, and the final amount, along with illustrative graphs.

exampleCalculation.title: Example of Compound Interest Calculation
exampleCalculation.scenario: Suppose you invest $ 1,000.00 with an annual interest rate of 12% for a period of 2 years, making a monthly contribution of $ 100.00.
exampleCalculation.calculationStep: By entering these values into the calculator and clicking Calculate Compound Interest, you will see the investment summary and corresponding graphs, allowing you to clearly visualize how your investment grows over time thanks to compound interest and monthly contributions. As shown in the image below:
exampleCalculation.resultImageAlt: Example of compound interest calculation result
exampleCalculation.conclusion: This example demonstrates how compound interest can significantly increase the value of your investment over time, especially with regular contributions.

definitionSection.title: What is Compound Interest?
definitionSection.intro: Compound interest calculates earnings on the principal plus accumulated interest, creating the "interest on interest" effect that drives growth over time.
definitionSection.example: Quick example: investing $ 100.00 at 10% per year, you end the first year with $ 110.00. In the second year, interest is applied to $ 110.00 and generates $ 11.00, closing the period with $ 121.00.
definitionSection.importance: This mechanism is ubiquitous in investments, loans, and financing, as it better reflects the real growth of capital than simple interest. For long cycles, the compound effect stands out and brings considerably higher returns.
definitionSection.compareLink: Need to compare with simple interest? Visit <link>simple interest calculator</link> to see the results.

comparativeSection.title: Difference Between Simple and Compound Interest
comparativeSection.intro: Simple interest always targets only the initial capital. Compound interest accumulates each gain and reinvests it, growing at an accelerated pace each period.
comparativeSection.example: Comparing: with 10% per year, simple interest yields $ 10.00 every year. With compound interest, the first month will yield the same amount as simple interest ($ 10.00), however, in the second month, the interest will already be applied to a higher amount ($ 110.00), so it will yield more ($ 11.00).
comparativeSection.conclusion: Although the difference seems small at the beginning, over time compound interest significantly outperforms simple interest. With our example, in the third year you would already have $ 133.10 with compound interest, while simple interest would yield only $ 30.00 in total. If we continue this example for 10 years, simple interest would give $ 100.00, while compound interest would result in $ 259.37.

formularySection.title: Compound Interest Formulas
formularySection.intro: There are two main expressions: one for growth with only the initial capital and another that includes recurring deposits.
formularySection.formula01Title: Compound Interest Amount Formula without Contributions
formularySection.formula01Intro: When you invest only the initial capital, the formula is:
formularySection.formula02Title: Compound Interest Amount Formula with Contributions
formularySection.formula02Intro: Adding constant contributions (PMT), the amount follows:
formularySection.formula02List02: The other symbols maintain the same meaning as in the previous formula.
formularySection.interestFormulaIntro: Interest is always calculated as the final amount minus the total invested (initial capital + sum of contributions). Therefore:

faqSection.question: 01. What are compound interest and how do they work in practice?
faqSection.answer: Compound interest works under the logic of 'interest on interest'. Unlike simple interest, the return of each period is added to the initial capital. In the following month or year, the new interest rate is calculated on this total value, making the final amount grow exponentially over time.

faqSection.question: 02. Is it possible to simulate scenarios with monthly contributions in the compound interest calculator?
faqSection.answer: Yes! Your calculator allows you to enter a monthly contribution amount. This means that in addition to the initial capital, you can simulate regular investments, which will further enhance the growth of your investment over time due to the compound effect.

faqSection.question: 03. Where are compound interest applied in the financial market?
faqSection.answer: They are the absolute rule of the market. In favor of your money, they are used in most investments (CDBs, Direct Treasury, funds and remunerated accounts). Against your pocket, they are the engine behind real estate financing, bank loans and credit card revolving payments, which explains why these debts grow so aggressively.

SimpleInterestCalculator.seoDescription: Calculate simple interest for loans and investments. Enter capital, rate, and period to see total invested, interest, and final amount with charts.

inputInterest.info: The interest rate is the percentage that will be applied to the initial capital during the period. It can be monthly or yearly.

inputPeriod.label: Period (t)

usageTutorial.title: How to Use the Simple Interest Calculator
usageTutorial.intro: To use the simple interest calculator, follow the steps below:
steps: Enter the initial capital you want to invest or lend in the Initial Capital ($) field.
Enter the interest rate in the Interest Rate (%) field. Select whether the rate is monthly or yearly from the dropdown menu next to it.
Set the period during which interest will be applied in the Period (t) field. Choose whether the period is in months or years from the corresponding dropdown menu.
Click the Calculate Simple Interest button to see the results.
usageTutorial.conclusion: After clicking the button, the calculator will display a summary of your investment, including the total invested, total interest, and final amount. In addition, graphs will be presented that illustrate the distribution of the amount and the evolution of the investment over time.

exampleCalculation.title: Example
exampleCalculation.intro: Suppose you invest $1,000.00 with an annual interest rate of 12% for a period of 2 years.
exampleCalculation.description: By entering these values into the calculator and clicking "Calculate Simple Interest", you will see the investment summary and corresponding graphs, allowing you to clearly visualize how your investment grows over time thanks to simple interest.
exampleCalculation.imageAlt2: Example of simple interest calculation result

definitionSection.title: What is Simple Interest?
definitionSection.intro: Simple interest calculates earnings only on the initial capital, without accumulating interest over time.
definitionSection.example: Quick example: investing $100.00 at 10% per year, you end the first year with $110.00. In the second year, interest is applied to $100.00 and generates $10.00, closing the period with $120.00.
definitionSection.limitations: This mechanism is not the most used in investments, loans, and financing, as it does not take advantage of the growth potential of compound interest. Additionally, it does not reflect the reality of many financial markets, where interest is capitalized periodically.
definitionSection.compareLink: Need to compare with compound interest? Visit our <link>compound interest calculator</link> to see the results side by side.

comparativeSection.title: Difference Between Simple and Compound Interest
comparativeSection.intro: Simple interest always targets only the initial capital. Compound interest accumulates each gain and reinvests it, growing at an accelerated pace each period.
comparativeSection.example: Comparing: with 10% per year, compound interest will yield more as time goes on. Meanwhile, simple interest will always yield the same amount annually. That is, if we invest $100.00 at 10% per year, in 10 years we will have: $200.00 total in simple interest versus $259.37 total in compound interest.
comparativeSection.conclusion: Even though the difference may seem small at first, over time compound interest significantly outperforms simple interest. With our example, in the third year you would already have $133.10 with compound interest, while simple interest would only yield $30.00 in total. If we continue this example for 10 years, simple interest would give $100.00, while compound interest would result in $259.37.

formularySection.title: Simple Interest Formulas
formularySection.intro: Unlike compound interest, we have more direct formulas to calculate the final amount in simple interest.
formularySection.formulaExplanation: With this formula, you can calculate the final amount (M) when investing an initial capital (C) at an interest rate (i) for a given number of periods (t).
formularySection.interestCalculation: Just as in compound interest, the interest is calculated by M - C, that is, the final amount minus the initial capital.
formularySection.interestFormula: J = C × i × t = M - C

faqSection.question: 01. What are simple interest and what is the main difference from compound interest?
faqSection.answer: In simple interest, the return rate is applied exclusively to the initial capital during the entire period. Unlike compound interest (where returns accelerate), in simple interest the earned value is always fixed and linear each month or year.

faqSection.question: 02. How to calculate the final amount and interest in simple interest?
faqSection.answer: To calculate the exact interest, simply multiply the initial capital, interest rate, and time (J = C × i × t). To discover the final amount, you add this generated interest to the original initial capital value (M = C + J).

faqSection.question: 03. When is the simple interest system used in the market?
faqSection.answer: Although compound interest dominates long-term investments, simple interest is still widely used in short-term financial operations, bill discounts, early receivables anticipation, and late payment penalty calculations.

QuadraticEquationCalculator.seoDescription: Solve quadratic equations step by step, view roots, delta, factorization, and interactive graphs for the parabola.
QuadraticEquationCalculator.alertWrongValues: Please enter valid numeric values for the coefficients, and make sure 'a' is not zero.
QuadraticEquationCalculator.alertMaxCharacters: The maximum allowed value is 9 characters.
QuadraticEquationCalculator.alertACannotBeZero: The coefficient 'a' cannot be zero in a quadratic equation.
QuadraticEquationCalculator.alertBCEmpty: Please enter valid numeric values for coefficients 'b' and 'c'.

inputCoefficientA.label: Coefficient a
inputCoefficientA.info: The coefficient 'a' is the value that multiplies the quadratic term (x²) in the quadratic equation. It determines the concavity of the parabola represented by the equation.

inputCoefficientB.label: Coefficient b
inputCoefficientB.info: The coefficient 'b' is the value that multiplies the linear term (x) in the quadratic equation. It influences the inclination of the parabola represented by the equation.

inputCoefficientC.label: Coefficient c
inputCoefficientC.info: The coefficient 'c' is the constant term in the quadratic equation. It determines the point where the parabola intersects the y-axis.

stepByStep.title: Step-by-step Solution
stepByStep.step1Content: In the equation {equation}, we have a = {a}, b = {b}, and c = {c}.
stepByStep.step2Content: Using the formula Δ = b² - 4ac to calculate the delta value, we have:
stepByStep.step4Content: Using the quadratic formula to find the roots:
stepByStep.step5Content: Analyze the roots found and interpret their meaning in the context of the problem.
stepByStep.conclusion: By following these steps, you will be able to solve any quadratic equation and find its roots.
stepByStep.deltaPositive: Since Δ > 0, the equation has two distinct real roots.
stepByStep.deltaZero: Since Δ = 0, the equation has one double real root.
stepByStep.deltaNegative: Since Δ < 0, the equation has no real roots (the roots are complex).

definitionSection.title: What is a Quadratic Equation?
definitionSection.intro: A quadratic equation, also known as a quadratic equation, is a polynomial equation of degree two that can be expressed in standard form:
definitionSection.symbolX: x represents the variable (unknown) we want to find.
definitionSection.explanation: Note that the coefficient a cannot be zero, because if it were, the polynomial equation would no longer be of the second degree and would become a first-degree (linear) equation.

rootsSection.title: How do Quadratic Equation Roots Work?
rootsSection.intro: The roots of a quadratic equation are the values of x that satisfy the equation ax² + bx + c = 0.
rootsSection.example: Quick example: In the equation x² - 5x + 6 = 0, the roots are x₁ = 2 and x₂ = 3, as substituting these values into the equation gives us 0.

resolutionGuide.title: Step-by-Step on How to Find the Roots of a Quadratic Equation
resolutionGuide.intro: Solving a quadratic equation involves finding the values of x that satisfy the equation in the standard form ax² + bx + c = 0. Here is a step-by-step guide to solve this type of equation:
resolutionGuide.step1: In the equation ax² + bx + c = 0, identify the values of a, b, and c.
resolutionGuide.step2: Use the formula Δ = b² - 4ac to calculate the delta value.
resolutionGuide.step3a: If Δ > 0, the equation has two distinct real roots.
resolutionGuide.step3b: If Δ = 0, the equation has one double real root.
resolutionGuide.step3c: If Δ < 0, the equation has no real roots (the roots are complex).
resolutionGuide.step4: Use the quadratic formula to find the roots:
resolutionGuide.step5: Analyze the roots found and interpret their meaning in the context of the problem.
resolutionGuide.conclusion: By following these steps, you will be able to solve any quadratic equation and find its roots.

usageTutorial.title: How to Use the Quadratic Equation Solver?
usageTutorial.intro: To use the Quadratic Equation Solver, follow the steps below:
usageTutorial.step1: Enter which coefficient a of the equation.
usageTutorial.step2: Enter which coefficient b of the equation. Coefficient b is responsible for the linear term.
usageTutorial.step3: Enter which coefficient c of the equation. Coefficient c is the constant term.
usageTutorial.step4: Click the "Calculate" button to see the results.
usageTutorial.conclusion: After clicking the button, the calculator will display results for the entered equation, including the equation roots, the graph, and other relevant information. Additionally, graphs will be presented that illustrate the shape of the parabola represented by the quadratic equation.

exampleCalculation.title: Example
exampleCalculation.scenario: Suppose you have the quadratic equation x² - 5x + 6 = 0.
exampleCalculation.description: By entering these values in the solver and clicking "Calculate Roots", you will obtain the roots x₁ = 2 and x₂ = 3, and the delta (Δ) = 1. Additionally, the graph of the parabola corresponding to the equation will also be displayed.
exampleCalculation.imageAlt2: Example of the quadratic equation result

graphSection.title: Graph of a Quadratic Equation
graphSection.intro: The graph of a quadratic equation is a parabola, which can open upward or downward depending on the value of coefficient 'a'. If 'a' is positive, the parabola opens upward; if 'a' is negative, the parabola opens downward.
graphSection.symmetry: The parabola is symmetric with respect to a vertical line called the axis of symmetry, which passes through the vertex of the parabola. This means that points equidistant from the axis of symmetry have the same y-value.
graphSection.vertex: Additionally, it is possible to identify important points on the graph, such as the vertex, which is the highest or lowest point of the parabola. The vertex can be calculated using the formulas:
graphSection.symbolAB: a, b — coefficients of the quadratic equation.

factorization.title: Factoring Quadratic Equations
factorization.intro: Factoring a quadratic equation is a method used to express the equation in the form of a product of two binomials. The factoring is of the form:
factorization.formula: ax² + bx + c = a(x - x₁)(x - x₂)
factorization.symbolABC: a, b, c — coefficients of the quadratic equation.
factorization.symbolRoots: x₁, x₂ — roots of the quadratic equation.
factorization.example01: Quick example: The equation x² - 5x + 6 = 0 factors as (x - 2)(x - 3) = 0. Note that the product (x - 2)(x - 3) equals 0 both if (x - 2) = 0 and if (x - 3) = 0, which makes it easy to see that the roots are x₁ = 2 and x₂ = 3.
factorization.example02: Another quick example: In addition to being easy to find roots in factored form, it is also possible to find the factored form from the roots. For example, if we have the roots x₁ = 4 and x₂ = -1, we can set up the factored form as (x - 4)(x + 1) = 0. For curiosity, expanding this product, we get the equation x² - 3x - 4 = 0.
factorization.conclusion: Factoring is a useful tool for solving quadratic equations, especially when the roots are rational numbers. It is also interesting to note that if the roots are complex, the factoring can be expressed in terms of complex numbers. Additionally, if the delta (Δ) is 0, the factoring will result in a squared binomial.

realWorld.title: Quadratic Equations in Real Life
realWorld.intro: Quadratic equations are widely used in various areas of real life, including physics, engineering, economics, and biology. They are particularly useful for modeling situations involving motion, growth, and optimization.
realWorld.examples: Some practical examples include: trajectories of objects in free fall, calculation of maximum areas, analysis of profits and losses, and modeling of biological populations. Quadratic equations help describe and predict complex behaviors in natural and artificial systems.

faqSection.question: 01. How to solve a quadratic equation using the Bhaskara Formula?
faqSection.answer: The classical method requires two steps: first, calculate the discriminant (Delta) using the formula Δ = b² - 4ac. Then, apply the Bhaskara Formula: x = (-b ± √Δ) / 2a. Our calculator shows exactly this step-by-step process, displaying how coefficients 'a', 'b', and 'c' interact to find real or complex roots.

faqSection.question: 02. What happens if the Delta (Δ) is negative or zero?
faqSection.answer: The value of Delta defines the type of root of the quadratic equation. If Δ > 0, the parabola crosses the X-axis twice (two distinct roots). If Δ = 0, the equation touches the X-axis at only one point (a double real root). If Δ < 0, the equation has no real roots, generating complex roots (the parabola floats without touching the X-axis).

faqSection.question: 03. How to discover the vertex of a parabola from a quadratic equation?
faqSection.answer: The vertex represents the point of maximum value (if the parabola opens downward) or minimum value (if it opens upward) of the graph. It is calculated directly from the coefficients and Delta using mathematical formulas: Vx = -b / 2a (for the X-axis) and Vy = -Δ / 4a (for the Y-axis).

PrivacyPolicy.title: Privacy Policy
PrivacyPolicy.intro: Your privacy is important to us. It is the policy of Decmatrix to respect your privacy regarding any information we may collect on the website and on other websites that we own and operate.
paragraphs: We request personal information only when we really need it to provide you with a service. We do this through fair and legal means, with your knowledge and consent. We also inform you why we are collecting and how it will be used.

We only retain information collected for as long as needed to provide the requested service. When we store data, we protect it within commercially acceptable means to prevent loss, theft, as well as unauthorized access, disclosure, copying, use or modification.
We do not share personally identifiable information publicly or with third parties, except where required by law.

Our website may have links to external websites that are not operated by us. Be aware that we have no control over the content and practices of these websites and cannot accept responsibility for their respective privacy policies.
You are free to refuse our request for personal information, understanding that we may not be able to provide some of the services you desire.
cookiesItems: The Google AdSense service we use to serve ads uses a DoubleClick cookie to display more relevant ads across the web and limit the number of times a specific ad is shown to you.
For more information about Google AdSense, please see the official Google AdSense privacy FAQs.

We use ads to offset the costs of running this website and fund future developments. The behavioral advertising cookies used by this website are designed to ensure we present the most relevant ads possible at all times.
Various partners advertise on our behalf and affiliate tracking cookies simply allow us to see if our users have accessed the website through one of our partner sites.
PrivacyPolicy.commitmentIntro: The user commits to making appropriate use of the contents and information that Decmatrix offers on the website, in an enunciative but not limited manner:

b) Not spread propaganda or content of a racist, xenophobic or anti-human rights nature;
c) Not cause damage to Decmatrix's physical (hardware) and logical (software) systems or introduce and spread computer viruses.

matrixInput.title: Matrix
matrixInput.notSquareMatrix: Matrix is not square. Determinant and inverse are not available.

matrixMultiplicationComponent.error: Error: The number of columns in Matrix A must be equal to the number of rows in Matrix B for multiplication.

matrixMultiplication.seoDescription: Multiply matrices of different dimensions with fraction support and detailed examples. Understand matrix multiplication step by step.

usageTutorial.title: How to use the Matrix Multiplier
usageTutorial.intro: To use the Matrix Multiplier, follow the steps below:
steps: Enter the size of the first matrix (Matrix A) in the Rows and Columns fields.
Enter the desired values in Matrix A in the corresponding fields, which can be integers or decimals. (Fractions are also allowed, such as 3/4)
Enter the size of the second matrix (Matrix B) in the Rows and Columns fields.
Enter the desired values in Matrix B in the corresponding fields, which can be integers or decimals. (Fractions are also allowed, such as 3/4)
usageTutorial.observation: Remember that the number of columns of Matrix A must be equal to the number of rows of Matrix B for multiplication to be possible.
usageTutorial.conclusion: The multiplier will provide the result of multiplying the matrices, allowing you to clearly visualize the product of the two matrices.

exampleCalculation.title: Example of Matrix Multiplication
exampleCalculation.scenario: Suppose you have two matrices, A and B, and you want to calculate their product.
exampleCalculation.calculationStep: When you enter the values of matrices A and B in the corresponding fields, you will see the result of multiplying the matrices, allowing you to clearly visualize the product of the two matrices. As shown in the image below:
exampleCalculation.conclusion: This example demonstrates how the matrix multiplier can be a useful tool for performing matrix multiplication calculations quickly and accurately, especially in contexts such as linear algebra, computer graphics, and data analysis.

definitionSection.title: Defining Matrix Multiplication
definitionSection.intro: Matrix multiplication is a fundamental operation in linear algebra that combines two matrices to produce a third matrix. Unlike basic operations, matrix multiplication involves summing the products of corresponding elements from rows of the first matrix and columns of the second matrix.
definitionSection.existence: Since matrix multiplication combines elements from two matrices, it has an existence condition: it is only possible when the number of columns of the first matrix is equal to the number of rows of the second matrix. If this condition is not met, matrix multiplication cannot be performed.

proprieties.title: Properties of Matrix Multiplication
list: Associative: (AB)C = A(BC) for any matrices A, B and C, provided the dimensions are compatible for multiplication.
Distributive: A(B + C) = AB + AC and (A + B)C = AC + BC for any matrices A, B and C, provided the dimensions are compatible for multiplication.
NNon-commutative: AB ≠ BA for any matrices A and B. This means that the order of matrices in multiplication is important and can affect the result.

Multiplicative Identity Property: There exists an identity element in matrix multiplication, which is the identity matrix. Multiplying any matrix by the identity matrix does not change the original matrix. That is, for any matrix A, we have AI = IA = A, where I is the identity matrix of compatible dimensions with A.

Multiplicative Null Element Property: There exists a null element in matrix multiplication, which is the null matrix. Multiplying any matrix by the null matrix results in the null matrix. That is, for any matrix A, we have A0 = 0A = 0, where 0 is the null matrix of compatible dimensions with A.
Dimension Property: Matrix multiplication respects the dimensions of the involved matrices. That is, if A is an m × n matrix and B is an n × p matrix, then their product AB will be an m × p matrix.

definitionSection.importance: This operation is essential in various fields, including physics, engineering, computer science, and data analysis. It is used to solve systems of linear equations, perform transformations in computer graphics, and analyze data in machine learning, among many other applications.

formularySection.title: Formulas for Matrix Multiplication
formularySection.intro: The formula for multiplying two matrices A and B, where A is an m × n matrix and B is an n × p matrix, is given by:

formulaList.formulaList01: Element of the resulting matrix C at position (i, j).
formulaList.formulaList02: Element of matrix A at position (i, k).
formulaList.formulaList03: Element of matrix B at position (k, j).
formulaList.formulaList04: Number of columns of A (equal to the number of rows of B).

formularySection.observation: Although it may seem complex, matrix multiplication is an operation that can be performed systematicall by following the formula above. With practice, you will be able to perform matrix multiplications quickly and accurately, which is especially useful in contexts such as linear algebra, computer graphics, and data science.

faqSection.question: 01. What is the main rule for multiplying two matrices?
faqSection.answer: The necessary condition for existence is that the number of columns of Matrix A must be exactly equal to the number of rows of Matrix B. If this rule is not met, the multiplication is mathematically impossible.

faqSection.question: 02. Is matrix multiplication commutative (A × B = B × A)?
faqSection.answer: No. Unlike multiplication of common numbers, in linear algebra the order of factors changes the product. Multiplying Matrix A by Matrix B usually generates a completely different result than multiplying Matrix B by Matrix A.

faqSection.question: 03. How is the step-by-step calculation of matrix multiplication done?
faqSection.answer: The calculation is not done element by element. You must multiply the elements of an entire row of the first matrix by the corresponding elements of a column of the second matrix, and then sum all these products to find a single value in the resulting matrix.

matrixBasicOperations.seoDescription: Perform matrix addition and subtraction with support for transposition and inversion. Work with fractions and visualize results clearly.

usageTutorial.title: How to Use Matrix Addition and Subtraction
usageTutorial.intro: To use the Matrix Addition and Subtraction tool, follow the steps below:
steps: Insert the size of the first matrix (Matrix A) in the Rows and Columns fields.
Insert the desired values in Matrix A in the corresponding fields. You can use integers or decimals (fractions are also allowed)
Insert the size of the second matrix (Matrix B) in the Rows and Columns fields.
Insert the desired values in Matrix B in the corresponding fields. You can use integers or decimals (fractions are also allowed)
usageTutorial.conclusion: The calculator will display the result of the addition and subtraction of the matrices, allowing you to clearly visualize the result of both matrices.

exampleCalculation.title: Example of Matrix Addition and Subtraction
exampleCalculation.scenario: Suppose you have two matrices, A and B, and you want to calculate their sum and difference.
exampleCalculation.imageAlt: Example of matrix addition and subtraction
exampleCalculation.calculationStep: By entering the values of matrices A and B in the corresponding fields, you will see the result of the addition and subtraction of the matrices, allowing you to clearly visualize the result of both matrices. As shown in the image below:
exampleCalculation.resultImageAlt: Example of matrix addition and subtraction
exampleCalculation.conclusion: This example demonstrates how the matrix calculator can be a useful tool for quickly and accurately performing matrix addition and subtraction, especially in contexts such as linear algebra, computer graphics, and data analysis.

stepByStep.title: Step-by-Step Calculation
stepByStep.intro: Addition and subtraction are performed element by element, keeping the same position in each matrix.

steps.title: Step 01: Identify the positions
steps.description: Each element of the matrix is identified by its position (row, column). For an n x n matrix, we have n² positions: a₁₁, a₁₂ ..., a₂₁, a₂₂,... for Matrix A and b₁₁, b₁₂, b₂₁, b₂₂,... for Matrix B.

steps.title: Step 02: Add each corresponding position
steps.description: Perform the operation element by element. In this example, we will call the result matrix c:

steps.title: For subtraction
steps.description: The same principle applies to subtraction:

steps.title: Step 03: Build the result matrix
steps.description: Arrange the calculated values in the same positions as the original matrix.

soma.title: Addition: A + B
steps: a₁₁ + b₁₁ = 2 + 6 = 8
a₁₂ + b₁₂ = 5 + 3 = 8
a₁₃ + b₁₃ = 3 + 4 = 7
a₂₁ + b₂₁ = 1 + 5 = 6
a₂₂ + b₂₂ = 4 + 2 = 6
a₂₃ + b₂₃ = 6 + 1 = 7
a₃₁ + b₃₁ = 7 + 2 = 9
a₃₂ + b₃₂ = 2 + 9 = 11
a₃₃ + b₃₃ = 8 + 3 = 11

subtracao.title: Subtraction: A - B
steps: a₁₁ - b₁₁ = 2 - 6 = -4
a₁₂ - b₁₂ = 5 - 3 = 2
a₁₃ - b₁₃ = 3 - 4 = -1
a₂₁ - b₂₁ = 1 - 5 = -4
a₂₂ - b₂₂ = 4 - 2 = 2
a₂₃ - b₂₃ = 6 - 1 = 5
a₃₁ - b₃₁ = 7 - 2 = 5
a₃₂ - b₃₂ = 2 - 9 = -7
a₃₃ - b₃₃ = 8 - 3 = 5

stepByStep.observation: If any position does not exist in one of the matrices, the operation is not valid.

errorArticle.title: Common Errors in Operations
errorArticle.intro: The most common error is trying to add or subtract matrices of different sizes. This is not allowed because there is no element-by-element correspondence.
errorArticle.example: Example: a 2×3 matrix cannot be added to a 3×2 matrix, as the positions (i, j) do not align.
errorArticle.conclusion: To avoid this error, the calculator forces the matrices to have the same dimensions before performing the operation.

definitionSection.title: Defining Matrix Addition and Subtraction
definitionSection.intro: Matrix addition and subtraction is a fundamental operation in linear algebra that combines two matrices of the same dimension to produce a third matrix. Unlike multiplication, matrix addition and subtraction involve adding or subtracting the corresponding elements of the rows and columns of the matrices.
definitionSection.existence: For it to be possible to add or subtract two matrices, they must have the same dimensions (the same number of rows and columns). If the dimensions are different, addition or subtraction cannot be performed.

proprieties.title: Properties of Matrix Addition and Subtraction
list: Associative: (A + B) + C = A + (B + C) for any matrices A, B, and C, as long as the dimensions are compatible for addition.
Commutative: A + B = B + A for any matrices A and B, as long as the dimensions are compatible for addition.
Identity element of addition: For any matrix A, there is a unique matrix 0 (zero matrix) such that A + 0 = 0 + A = A.
Additive inverse property: For each A, there is a unique matrix B such that A + B = B + A = 0. Matrix B is called the additive inverse of A and is denoted by -A.
Closure property of addition: The matrix A + B has the same dimensions as A and B.

definitionSection.importance: Matrix addition is essential in several areas, including computer graphics, data analysis, and solving linear systems. Matrix subtraction is equally important, as it allows comparing and analyzing differences between data sets represented by matrices.

faqSection.question: 01. It is possible to add or subtract matrices of different sizes?
faqSection.answer: NNo. For addition or subtraction to be possible, both matrices must have exactly the same dimensions (same number of rows and columns). For example, it is not possible to add a 2x3 matrix with a 3x2 matrix.

faqSection.question: 02. How does the calculation of matrix addition and subtraction work?
faqSection.answer: Unlike multiplication, addition and subtraction are direct operations. The calculation is done element by element. You simply add or subtract the number that is in the exact position (i, j) of the first matrix with the number in the same position of the second matrix.

faqSection.question: 03. Does matrix addition have the commutative property?
faqSection.answer: Yes. In matrix addition, the order does not matter. The sum of Matrix A with Matrix B (A + B) will produce exactly the same result as the sum of Matrix B with Matrix A (B + A).

bubbleSort.description: Bubble Sort is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly walks through the list to be sorted, comparing each pair of adjacent elements and swapping them if they are in the wrong order. This process is repeated until the list is sorted. The name 'Bubble Sort' comes from the fact that larger elements 'bubble' to the top of the list with each iteration.
bubbleSort.complexity: Complexity: In the classic version, the time complexity is always O(n²) in all scenarios (worst, average, and best case). Since this algorithm has no early-stop mechanism, even if the list is already completely sorted (best case), it will still perform all passes and comparisons in a 'blind' way, wasting processing time.

bubbleSortOtimized.description: The optimized Bubble Sort is a variation of the Bubble Sort algorithm that includes a check to determine whether the list is already sorted. If during a full pass through the list no swaps occur, the algorithm can conclude that the list is sorted and stop immediately, avoiding unnecessary passes. This can significantly improve performance in cases where the list is almost sorted or already sorted.
bubbleSortOtimized.complexity: Complexity: In the worst case (reverse-ordered list) and in the average case, the time complexity remains O(n²). However, the big performance gain occurs in the best case (already sorted list): the algorithm makes only one initial pass, realizes that no swap was needed, and immediately stops execution (using 'break'). This results in a time complexity of O(n), making it extremely efficient for lists that are already almost sorted.

insertion.description: Insertion Sort is a sorting algorithm that builds the sorted portion of the list one element at a time. At each step it removes an element from the unsorted part of the array and inserts it in the correct position inside the already sorted part, shifting the other elements to the right.
insertion.complexity: Complexity: In the worst and average cases (reverse or random order), the time complexity is O(n²). In the best case (already sorted list), the algorithm only walks through the list once without needing to shift elements, resulting in O(n) time complexity. Like Bubble Sort, it is an in-place algorithm with O(1) space complexity.

selection.description: Selection Sort is a simple sorting algorithm that divides the array into two parts: a sorted part and an unsorted part. In each iteration, it scans the unsorted part to find the smallest element and swaps it with the first element of the unsorted part, effectively moving it to the sorted part. This process is repeated until the entire array is sorted.
selection.complexity: Complexity: Unlike Bubble Sort and Insertion Sort, Selection Sort is not adaptive. It does not know if the list is already sorted and will always perform a complete scan. Therefore, its time complexity is O(n²) in all scenarios (worst, average, and best case). Its only significant advantage over the other two algorithms is the reduced number of swaps in memory (at most O(n) swaps). It is an in-place algorithm with O(1) space complexity.

ui.inputInfo: Enter up to 21 integer numbers from -999 to 999, separated by commas.
ui.errorDuplicateComma: There seems to be a missing element (a duplicate comma somewhere perhaps).
ui.errorInvalidNegative: There seems to be an invalid negative sign (double '-' detected).
ui.errorTooManyNumbers: Please enter 21 numbers or fewer to avoid visual clutter.
ui.errorAtLeastOneNumber: Please enter at least one valid number.
ui.stepsFallbackMessage: Run the algorithm to see the recorded steps.

classic.init: Starting classic Bubble Sort. Array size: {n}.
classic.compare: Comparing: Is the value {left} greater than {right}?
classic.noSwap: No. The values are already in the correct order.
classic.endPass: End of pass {pass}. The largest element has moved to the end.

optimized.init: Starting optimized Bubble Sort. Array size: {n}.
optimized.compare: Comparing: Is the value {left} greater than {right}?
optimized.noSwap: No. The values are already in the correct order.
optimized.endPassSwapped: End of pass. Were there swaps? Yes. Continue.
optimized.endPassNoSwap: End of pass. Were there swaps? No! The array is already sorted (Break).

insertion.startPass: Picking the element at position {pass} (value {value}) to insert into the sorted part.
insertion.compare: Comparing: is the value {left} greater than the element we are inserting ({current})?
insertion.shift: Yes. Shifting the value {shifted} one position to the right.
insertion.noShift: No. We found the point where the value on the left ({left}) is not greater than the current element.
insertion.reachedStart: We reached the beginning of the array. The element will be inserted in the first position.
insertion.finished: Array fully sorted with Insertion Sort!

selection.startPass: Pass {pass}: the value {min} is defined as the provisional smallest element.
selection.compare: Current value: {current}. Is it smaller than the provisional minimum ({min})?
selection.newMin: Yes! {newMin} is the new provisional smallest element.
selection.checkSwap: End of scan. Is the smallest found ({min}) in the position of the initial element ({target})?
selection.swap: NNo! Performing swap: {min} goes to its definitive position.
selection.noSwap: Sim. {min} já está na posição correta. Nenhuma troca necessária.
selection.finished: Array ordenado com sucesso pelo Selection Sort!

bubbleSort.seoDescription: Watch the Bubble Sort algorithm step by step with animated bars, properties, and usage explanations.

definition.title: Definition of Bubble Sort
definition.content: Bubble Sort is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly walks through the list to be sorted. It compares each pair of adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. The process is repeated until the entire list is sorted. The name 'Bubble Sort' comes from the fact that larger elements 'bubble' to the end of the list with each iteration.

howWorks.title: How Bubble Sort Works
steps: Start by comparing the first two elements of the list.
If the first element is greater than the second, swap them.
Move to the next elements and repeat the process.
Continue until you have walked through the entire list without needing to make any more swaps.
The list is sorted when no more swaps are needed.

contextAndUsage.title: Applications and Real-World Use
contextAndUsage.content: Although it is one of the most famous and studied algorithms in programming, Bubble Sort is rarely implemented in production systems, real-world software, or standard libraries. Its quadratic nature O(n²) makes it extremely slow as the amount of data grows. Its main value is didactic: it is the perfect tool to introduce students to sorting concepts, array iteration, and performance analysis, serving as an essential foundation for understanding more efficient algorithms.

proprieties.title: Properties of Bubble Sort
list: Time complexity: The worst and average cases of Bubble Sort have a time complexity of O(n²), where n is the number of elements in the list. The best case, when the list is already sorted, has a time complexity of O(n) due to the need to walk through the list to verify that it is sorted.

Space complexity: Bubble Sort is an in-place sorting algorithm, which means it does not require significant additional space beyond what is needed to store the original list. Therefore, its space complexity is O(1).

Stability: Bubble Sort is a stable sorting algorithm, which means it preserves the relative order of equal elements. If two elements have the same value, they will remain in the same order as in the original list after sorting.

faqSection.question: 01. Why is Bubble Sort considered an inefficient algorithm?
faqSection.answer: Because it requires repeatedly comparing adjacent elements many times, even when many of them are already close to the correct position. In a very large list, the number of operations grows quadratically (n²), which can drastically slow down processing.

faqSection.question: 02. What is the difference between Classic and Optimized Bubble Sort?
faqSection.answer: Classic Bubble Sort always performs all possible passes, even if the list has become sorted halfway through the process. The Optimized version has a 'flag' (a sentinel variable) that checks whether any swap has been made in the last pass. If no swap occurred, it understands that the list is perfectly sorted and immediately stops the algorithm, saving time.

faqSection.question: 03. Does Bubble Sort need extra memory to run?
faqSection.answer: No. One of its few technical advantages is being an in-place algorithm. It needs only a minimal space in memory (a single temporary variable) to hold a number while swapping the positions of two elements.

faqSection.question: 04. When should I choose Bubble Sort in a real project?
faqSection.answer: In practice, almost never. Native language algorithms (such as JavaScript or Python's .sort()) use much faster methods under the hood (such as TimSort). Bubble Sort would only be acceptable on tiny lists where the absolute simplicity of the code matters more than execution speed.

insertionSort.seoDescription: Visualize how Insertion Sort builds the sorted array step by step, with explanations and properties.

definition.title: Definition of Insertion Sort
definition.content: Insertion Sort is a simple sorting algorithm that builds the sorted list progressively, one element at a time. It works very similarly to the way you would organize playing cards in your hands: you take a new card from the unsorted pile and insert it in the exact position among the cards that are already in order in your hand.

howWorks.title: How Insertion Sort Works
steps: Assume that the first element of the list (index 0) is already in its final, sorted position.
Take the next element
Compare this element with the previous ones (which are already sorted), moving from right to left.
While the previous element is greater than the current card, swap their positions to push the larger values to the right.
The current card will find its correct spot. Repeat this process for all remaining items in the list.

contextAndUsage.title: Applications and Real-World Use
contextAndUsage.content: Unlike Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort is very useful in the real world. Although it is inefficient for huge and completely unsorted lists, it is incredibly fast for small lists (fewer than 50 items) or data that is already almost sorted. Because of this surgical efficiency, languages like Python and Java use hybrid algorithms (such as TimSort) that break huge lists into small chunks and trigger Insertion Sort under the hood to sort these fragments at lightning speed.

proprieties.title: Properties of Insertion Sort
list: Time complexity: The worst case (reversed list) and the average case have a time complexity of O(n²). However, its big advantage shines in the best case: when the list is already sorted or almost sorted, the complexity drops to O(n), since it makes only one comparison per element and almost no swaps.

Space complexity: It is an in-place algorithm. It reorders the elements by moving them inside the original array itself, needing only one temporary variable for swaps. Therefore, its space complexity is O(1).
Stability: Insertion Sort is a stable algorithm. If two elements have the same value, the algorithm will not swap their positions, perfectly preserving their original relative order.

faqSection.question: 01. Is Insertion Sort better than Bubble Sort?
faqSection.answer: Yes. Although both belong to the O(n²) complexity category in the worst case, Insertion Sort performs considerably fewer comparisons and movements in practice. In real scenarios, it is almost always faster and more efficient than Bubble Sort and Selection Sort.

faqSection.question: 02. What does it mean to say that it is an 'adaptive' algorithm?
faqSection.answer: It means that its performance adapts and improves drastically according to the current state of the list. If you give it a huge list with only two numbers out of place, Insertion Sort will 'skip' the part that is already correct and fix only the defect, running almost at the maximum speed of O(n).

faqSection.question: 03. Why does it always start scanning from the second element (index 1)?
faqSection.answer: Because, mathematically speaking, a sublist made up of a single element is already considered perfectly sorted. The algorithm assumes that index 0 is the correct base and then starts inserting the remaining elements around it.

faqSection.question: 04. When should I choose to use Insertion Sort in my projects?
faqSection.answer: You should use it in two main situations: when the amount of data is very small, or when you have a system that receives new data one by one (such as a continuous stream) and needs to insert them into a database or list that is already sorted.

selectionSort.seoDescription: Follow how Selection Sort repeatedly selects the smallest element, with interactive explanation and properties.

definition.title: Definition of Selection Sort
definition.content: Selection Sort divides the list into two parts: a sorted part (on the left) and an unsorted part (on the right). On each iteration, it finds the smallest element in the unsorted part and moves it to the end of the sorted part. This process repeats until the entire list is sorted.

howWorks.title: How Selection Sort Works
steps: Consider the first element of the unsorted part as the current minimum.
Scan all elements to the right, comparing each one against the current minimum.
If a smaller value is found, it becomes the new current minimum.
After the scan, perform a single swap: place the smallest value found at the first position of the unsorted part.
Advance the sorted boundary one position and repeat until the end of the array.

contextAndUsage.title: Applications and Real-World Usage
contextAndUsage.content: Like Bubble Sort, Selection Sort is rarely used in production due to its quadratic complexity. Its main advantage is performing the minimum possible number of swaps — at most N−1. This makes it useful in constrained hardware environments, such as flash memory (EEPROM), where each write operation consumes processing power and physically degrades the chip.

proprieties.title: Properties of Selection Sort
list: Time complexity: O(n²) in all cases — best, average, and worst. Because it is not adaptive, the algorithm always scans the entire list on every pass, even if it is already sorted.
Space complexity: O(1). It is an in-place algorithm: elements are reordered within the original array, using only a single temporary variable for the swap.
Stability: Not stable. The long-distance swap can reverse the relative order of two elements with equal values.

faqSection.question: 01. What is the main difference between Selection Sort and Insertion Sort?
faqSection.answer: Insertion Sort is adaptive: if the list is already nearly sorted, it finishes much faster. Selection Sort is not adaptive — it always performs O(n²) comparisons, scanning the entire list on every pass regardless of the initial order of the data.

faqSection.question: 02. If it is always O(n²), why learn this algorithm?
faqSection.answer: For its educational value. It clearly illustrates the difference between the number of comparisons (many) and the number of memory swaps (very few) — an important concept for understanding algorithmic trade-offs.

faqSection.question: 03. Why is classic Selection Sort not stable?
faqSection.answer: Consider the list [4a, 4b, 1]. On the first pass, the algorithm finds the smallest element (1) and swaps it with the first element (4a), resulting in [1, 4b, 4a]. The 4a, which appeared before 4b, now comes after it — the relative order of equal elements has been broken.

faqSection.question: 04. Does Selection Sort require extra memory?
faqSection.answer: No. It is in-place: it operates directly on the original array without allocating any auxiliary structures. Memory usage is O(1).
